public boolean makeStringsEqual(String s, String target) {

   if (isAllZeros(target)) {

      return isAllZeros(s);

   } else {

      if (isAllZeros(s)) return false;

      if (s.length() == 2) {

}

private boolean isAllZeros(String str) {

   for (char c : str.toCharArray()) {

      if (c == '1') return false;

   }

private int countOnes(String str) {

   int count = 0;

   for (char c : str.toCharArray()) {

      if (c == '1') count++;

   }

   return count;

}

But in Java, since we can't have helper functions inside the Solution class in the starter code, we need to inline these checks.

Alternatively, we can compute them directly in the code.

So, inside the makeStringsEqual function:

First, check if target is all zeros. How?

We can loop through each character in target.

Similarly for s.

So:

public boolean makeStringsEqual(String s, String target) {

   // Check if target is all zeros.

   boolean targetAllZero = true;

   for (int i = 0; i < target.length(); i++) {

      if (target.charAt(i) != '0') {

         targetAllZero = false;

         break;

      }

   }

   if (targetAllZero) {

      // Check if s is all zeros.

      for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {

         if (s.charAt(i) != '0') {

             return false;

         }

      }

      return true;

   }

   // Target is not all zeros. Check if s has at least one '1'.

   boolean sAllZero = true;

   for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {

      if (s.charAt(i) == '1') {

         sAllZero = false;

         break;

      }

   }

   if (sAllZero) {

      return false;

   }

   // Now check if n == 2 and both s and target have exactly one '1' and are different.

   if (s.length() == 2) {

      int countS = 0;

      for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {

         if (c == '1') countS++;

      }

      int countTarget = 0;

      for (char c : target.toCharArray()) {

         if (c == '1') countTarget++;

      }

      if (countS == 1 && countTarget == 1 && !s.equals(target)) {

         return false;

      }

   }

   return true;

}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean makeStringsEqual(String s, String target) {
        boolean targetAllZero = true;
        for (int i = 0; i < target.length(); i++) {
            if (target.charAt(i) != '0') {
                targetAllZero = false;
                break;
            }
        }
        if (targetAllZero) {
            for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
                if (s.charAt(i) != '0') {
                    return false;
                }
            }
            return true;
        }
        
        boolean sAllZero = true;
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            if (s.charAt(i) == '1') {
                sAllZero = false;
                break;
            }
        }
        if (sAllZero) {
            return false;
        }
        
        if (s.length() == 2) {
            int countS = 0;
            for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
                if (c == '1') countS++;
            }
            int countTarget = 0;
            for (char c : target.toCharArray()) {
                if (c == '1') countTarget++;
            }
            if (countS == 1 && countTarget == 1 && !s.equals(target)) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution checks if the target can be achieved by analyzing the presence of 1s in both strings. If the target is all zeros, s must also be all zeros. If s has no 1s but target has at least one, it's impossible. For n=2, special handling is required when both
